observations.recommendation:
To address the deficiencies and mitigate the associated risks, we recommended that
the Management agreed to take the following actions:
a. Strengthen the internal control system by documenting and implementing
specific control activities related to cash management, in accordance with
the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, including:
o Roles and Responsibilities: Clearly define the duties of collectors, cashier,
liquidating officer, the treasurer, the accountant and other personnel.
o Step-by-Step Procedures/ Processes: Provide detailed instructions for
collections, remittances, deposits, and reconciliations.
o Timelines: Specify deadlines for remittances, deposits, and reconciliations.
o Control Mechanisms: Include the conduct of random audits on collections
and deposits, surprise cash counts, evaluation of the effectiveness of the
policies and training sessions / reorientation to personnel involved in cash
management.
o Sanctions for Non-Compliance: Outline penalties for violations of the
policies (e.g., delayed remittances, unsupported disbursements) with a
graduated system like warnings or suspensions to ensure adherence and
accountability.
b. Direct all collecting officers to remit collections in full and prepare the RCD
at the close of each business day to ensure complete and accurate recording
and reporting of transactions by the Accounting Office. Moreover, direct the
Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all collections to AGDB in accordance
with Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
c. Ensure that reconciliations of cash balances are conducted between the
accountable officer and the accountant at least quarterly, or more frequently
if required by management.

To address the noted deficiencies and prevent similar occurrences in the future, we
recommended and Management agreed to undertake the following actions:
a. The Municipal Accountant is advised to strictly apply the accrual basis of
accounting in accordance with IPSAS 1, ensuring that all expenses are
recognized in the period in which they are incurred, irrespective of when
payment occurs.
b. Issue a formal memorandum to all departments, setting clear guidelines and
deadlines for the submission of claims, especially towards the year-end
c. Establish a periodic internal review or audit of expense claims to verify that
they are submitted on time and supported by complete documentation.
d. The Municipal Accountant is advised to adjust the erroneous recording of
expenses to reflect the appropriate period. The following journal entry is
recommended:

Expenses 393,087.87

The audit of MGL’s PPE management revealed several significant deficiencies that
undermine the reliability of the reported ₱262,596,592.59 million in net book value. In
particular, we identified issues with physical inventory. The following specific
observations outline this issue:
Non-Conduct of Annual Physical inventory count
Yearly inventory-taking is an indispensable procedure that, apart from checking
the integrity of custodianship, ensures that the PPE reflected in the financial
statement exists and is working according to its intended purpose. Likewise, the
physical stock taking of equipment also serves as a basis for the preparation of
relevant reports, which, at the end of each quarter, the Accounting and the
General Service Office (GSO) reconcile to ascertain its completeness. The Local
Chief Executive (LCE) is required to conduct the physical inventory of all
equipment in the offices at least once a year.1 Consequently, regardless of the
mode of acquisition, the Head of the Agency must create an Inventory Committee
which is composed of an adequate number of members in order to complete the
task.2 As a minimum requirement, however, the inventory Committee must have
at least one member each from the Accounting and General Services Unit of the
LGU.
Moving forward, as part of the inventory procedure, the head of the GSO shall
prepare the Inventory Guidelines and must forward the same to the Agency Head
for approval. The formulated plans must specify the date of physical inventory,
the roles of each personnel, and the mode of counting that will be used.3 After
which, the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment
(RPCPPE) shall be prepared by the Inventory Committee to report the physical
count of PPE by type, such as heavy equipment, technical and scientific
equipment, motor vehicles, office equipment, furniture and fixtures. It shall be
prepared yearly in three (3) copies as certified correct by the Inventory Committee
and approved by the agency head. After its completion, reports must be rendered
and furnished to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.
When we reviewed the documents related to the inventory taking, we noted that
an Inventory and Disposal Committee was constituted by the management. The
Committee’s main objective is to conduct a physical inventory to render
Inventory Reports and facilitate the disposition of all unserviceable properties.
Nonetheless, our inquiry with the management somewhat revealed that the above-
mentioned Committee has not conducted any Physical Inventory and Disposal
ever since it was constituted. To give their explanation, the members of the
Committee have reasoned out that the non-performance of their functions as
members of the Committee was caused by the heavy workloads that they have
been attending to in their respective offices, hence they were not able to render
the required inventory reports.
In response thereto, the Audit Team has emphasized the importance of
immediately conducting the physical inventory to ascertain any discrepancies and
employ measures to correct them and to ensure the accuracy of the recorded
balance. While we do understand that the members of the Inventory Committee
are attending to various workloads of equal importance, we are pointing out,
however, that without any action from the constituted committee, the PPE balance
will always be misrepresented. The role of the committee is so vital that it will
become the turning point for the LGU to start anew in terms of its PPE
management.
These observations point to systemic and process-related issues within the MGL. Based
on audit inquiries and evaluation, several key systemic issues have contributed to the
deficiencies in MGL’s PPE management:
1. Inadequate Internal Control Framework and Oversight
• Lack of Dedicated Structures: The absence of a formal Inventory Committee and
weak interdepartmental coordination have severely impacted the ability to
perform complete physical counts.
• Opportunities for Strengthened Leadership Oversight: Leadership oversight can
be enhanced to support more consistent adherence to internal control policies and
NGAS Manual requirements.
2. Deficient Documentation and Record-Keeping Processes
• Incomplete Record Maintenance: The failure to maintain comprehensive PPE
ledger cards and Property cards has resulted in significant gaps in asset records,
delaying physical counts and reconciliation efforts.
3. Lack of Accountability Mechanisms
• Ongoing Organizational Weakness: The identified deficiencies have persisted
across multiple reporting periods without effective accountability structures. The
lack of clear accountability has led to minimal corrective action and enforcement
of compliance measures.
4. Historical and Process Gaps
• Carry-Forward Issues: The ongoing presence of unresolved PPE discrepancies
from previous reporting periods highlights a persistent failure to resolve internal
control and documentation deficiencies.
• Retention of Documentation: The non-retention of critical documents for
22
depreciation computation further compounds the challenges in reconciling and
accurately reporting PPE values.
The existence of deficiencies in PPE account balances has become a perennial issue for
MGL, undermining the accuracy of its financial statements. Inability to conduct complete
physical counts, incomplete record-keeping, and improper depreciation recognition
introduce significant uncertainties regarding asset existence, completeness, and valuation.
If these issues are not properly addressed, they will lead to exceptions in the fair
presentation of MGL's financial position and deprive management of the reliable and
useful information necessary for effective decision-making and accountability.
To address these issues decisively, we recommended and Management agreed to
take corrective actions. This includes:
a. Conduct One-Time Cleansing of PPE Accounts:
Implement COA Circular 2020-006 to resolve concerns related to PPE
accounts' accuracy, existence, and valuation. This one-time cleansing process will involve
the following:
• Conducting complete physical counts.
• Recognizing PPE items found at the station.
• Disposing of non-existent/missing PPE items.
b. To ensure these issues do not recur, management must enhance accountability
within key departments by establishing disciplinary or corrective measures if
responsibilities are not met. In particular, the Accounting Office, Property
Office, and Inventory Committee must strictly adhere to both IPSAS guidelines
for accurate financial reporting and NGAS Sections 119-124 for effective
physical inventory management and documentation. Key steps include:
• Clearly defining and monitoring responsibilities: Develop a detailed
responsibilities matrix that specifies each officer’s duties, reporting lines, and
deadlines. This should include defined performance indicators related to
asset record-keeping and inventory management.
• Instituting regular reviews and audits: Schedule quarterly internal audits
and monthly progress meetings to verify compliance with IPSAS and NGAS
requirements. This process should involve cross-checking physical inventory
counts against recorded data and documenting all findings for immediate
corrective action.
• Enforcing disciplinary actions: Establish a structured disciplinary
framework that outlines specific consequences for non-compliance. This
should include immediate corrective measures, mandatory retraining for
recurring issues, and escalation procedures to management for unresolved
deficiencies.

To address the foregoing observations and strengthen the financial management
practices of the Municipal Government of Lutayan, we recommended and
Management agreed to take the following actions:
a. Require the requesting unit/section to ensure completeness of the supporting
documents, including the approved Allotment and Obligation Slip (ALOBS)
from the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant before
requesting payment.
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the completeness of
supporting documents from the requesting unit/section before certifying the
disbursement vouchers.
c. Secure the approval of the Local Chief Executive before processing payment
for the disbursement.
d. Designate authorized official to approve regularly and/or recurring
administrative expenses to facilitate and reduce the workload of the LCE such
as: payrolls for regular or permanent employees, expenses for light, water,
telephone and telegraph services, remittances to government creditor agencies
such as GSIS, BIR, PHILHEALTH, LBP, DBP, NPO, PS of the DBM and
others.
e. Submit all financial reports such as paid Disbursement Vouchers (DVs), copies
of perfected Contracts and Purchase Orders (POs), Report on Collections and
Deposits (RCD), Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS), Bank Statements,
Report of Checks Issued (RCI), Statement of Appropriations, Allotments,
Obligations and Balances (SAAOB), and other required schedules to the audit
team within the prescribe period.

To ensure the timely and accurate disposition of long-outstanding and dormant
accounts and enhance the reliability of financial statements, we recommended and
Management agreed, through the Office of the Provincial Accountant as lead, to
take the following actions:
a. Immediately prioritize the review and analysis of all long-outstanding and
dormant balances. This involves collating necessary documents and initiating the
process for write-off requests to the Audit Team, if warranted, in accordance
with applicable accounting policies and regulations for the ongoing disposition of
dormant accounts. This process should also include adherence to COA Circular
No. 93-404 for any instances where documents evidencing financial transactions
or records of accountabilities are confirmed to be lost.
b. Consistently ensure the regular and timely submission of SDAs to the
auditor's office for verification. While COA Circular No. 2023-008 provided a
framework for a one-time cleansing, the principles of identifying and
addressing such accounts remain an ongoing responsibility for maintaining
financial statement reliability.
c. Implement effective control activities to ensure that all current and future
transactions are properly documented. This will prevent the recurrence of
dormant balances.

In view of the above observations and their significant impact on the integrity of the
Municipality’s financial reporting, it is strongly recommended and Management
agreed to take the following immediate actions:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant’s Office to strictly adhere to the NGAS
Manual for LGUs by diligently maintaining complete and up-to-date books
of original entry (journals) and final entry (ledgers) for all financial
transactions.
b. Require that all financial accounts, particularly those related to assets and
liabilities, are supported by regularly updated and reconciled schedules,
which must be made available for audit purposes.
c. Implement and continuously enhance accounting systems and procedures to
support the accurate and timely collection of evidence needed for financial
reporting. This includes establishing clear internal guidelines and stricter
enforcement of deadlines for the submission of complete supporting
documents, such as disbursement vouchers, by all concerned departments.
d. Conduct training sessions for the Municipal Accountant and staff to enhance
their understanding of NGAS requirements and the proper maintenance of
accounting records. The training should focus on practical application and
best practices, ensuring the team can handle the records with competence
and diligence.
e. Establish a formal turnover process for key accounting positions to ensure
knowledge transfer and operational continuity. Provide regular training for
the Municipal Accountant and accounting staff on NGAS policies and
procedures, emphasizing hands-on application and best practices in record-
keeping.
f. The Municipal Accountant's Office should assess its current staffing levels
and consider increasing personnel to effectively manage both Municipal and
Barangay accounting records. This will help to distribute the workload more
evenly and ensure all accounting records are accurately maintained.
g. To reduce processing delays, management should formally delegate
authority to a designated official for approving recurring administrative
expenses (such as payrolls, utility bills, and government remittances).
This delegation must be documented and communicated to all relevant
offices to ensure smooth and timely transaction processing.
h. Strengthen internal controls within the accounting system to ensure the
integrity and accuracy of financial records. Regular reviews and supervisory
checks should be implemented to monitor the effectiveness of the record-
keeping process and ensure timely and accurate submissions of supporting
documents.
i. Prioritize the preparation of financial statements that are accurate,
verifiable, and faithfully represent the Municipality’s financial position.
Compliance with IPSAS 1 and the IPSAS Conceptual Framework will
improve the reliability, transparency, and overall quality of financial
reporting.

b. Inclusion of Semi-Expendable Property in the balance of PPE
Tangible items below the capitalization threshold of P50,000.00 shall be accounted
for as semi-expendable property. The semi-expendable properties shall be reclassified to
the affected appropriate semi-expendable property accounts (if not yet issued to end-
user), expense accounts (if issued within the year), or prior period adjustment and
government equity (if issued in prior years). Review of the submitted accounting records
and disbursement vouchers revealed that semi-expendable inventories costing below
P50,000.00 with estimated useful life of more than one (1) year were taken up as PPE in
the accounting books. Details of the following semi-expendable inventories are as
follows:
Table D – List of Semi Expendable Property included as part of PPE in the
Financial Statements
Inventory Item General SEF Trust
Fund Fund
Other Machinery and Equipment P11,760.00 P0.00 P0.00
Accumulated Depreciation (7,056.00) - -
Other Machinery and Equipment net of P4,704.00 P0.00 P0.00
Accumulated Depreciation
When inquired about the observations noted, the management proffered that they
thought the circular regarding the increase in capitalization threshold of PPE is to be
treated prospectively, thus they weren’t able to reclassify the above property to semi
expendable expense or adjustment to the balance of government equity. Nevertheless,
they promised to revisit all their records regarding their PPE and make the necessary
adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts.
The Audit Team would like to emphasize that while the semi-expendable properties
are expense on the accounting side, necessary controls should still be in place to ensure
that said property are properly accounted and maintained.
During the exit conference, the management offered that the above equipment has
been long outstanding in the books. Accordingly, record of said property cannot be
located, hence it still remained in the books of the LGU. The audit team then stresses the
importance of conducting an inventory count so as to establish the records of the LGU.
The management made an assurance that they will undertake the necessary steps to
establish the record of its PPE.

We recommended and the management agreed to require the Municipal
Accounting Office, Designated Property Officer and the Inventory Committee to
undertake the necessary steps to establish record of the above property and consider
the recording of semi-expendable item/s in the respective inventory/expense
accounts, eliminating the same from the PPE balances, and the corresponding
accumulated depreciation; and issue the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip
(ICS) to all users of the semi-expendable item/s, if warranted.

A.3 Realignment of Funds for COVID-19-Related PPAs
Presidential Proclamation No. 992, s. 2020 was issued on March 9, 2020 declaring
State of Public Health Emergency throughout the country due to the outbreak of Corona
Virus Disease 2019 (COVID19). Subsequently, on March 16, 2020, Presidential
Proclamation No. 929, s. 2020 was issued declaring the entire nation under the State of
Calamity. Both Proclamations enjoined all government agencies and LGUs to render full
assistance and cooperation with each other and to mobilize the necessary resources to
undertake critical, urgent, and appropriate disaster response and measures to eliminate or
lessen the effect of COVID-19. Considering the foregoing, it was highly imperative to
provide additional guidelines on the use of the 20% DF to give the LGUs greater leeway
and flexibility in the utilization of funds for disaster preparedness and response efforts to
contain the spread of COVID-19, and to provide basic services to the affected population.
Consequently, DILG-DBM JMC No. 01 was issued on March 27, 2020 enumerating the
allowable COVID-19-related expenses that may be charged against the 20% DF. As
provided therein, the additional PPAs to be funded by the LGUs should be part of their
respective approved AIPs. In case such COVID-19 related PPAs are not among those
included in the approved AIP, the Local Development Council shall prepare a supplemental
investment program, to be approved by the local sanggunian.

In view of the above manifestations, the management opt to forego some of their
initially planned development projects to provide additional resources on disaster related
PPAs that are essential in addressing the threat brought by the pandemic. Accordingly, the
Municipality has realigned ₱17,472,839.00 or 43% of its development projects for
COVID-19-related PPAs. Summary of the realignment are presented hereunder:
Table E – Summary of Realigned PPAs
From Amount
20% Development Fund for CY 2020 ₱ 17,472,839.00
TOTAL 17,472,839.00
To Amount
Procurement of Medicines and Vitamins ₱ 1,000,000.00
Procurement of Equipment and Supplies for Municipal Isolation Area 1,000,000.00
Procurement of Relief Goods (Food Packs) 12,972,839.00
Food and Travel Expenses for Front liners 1,000,000.00
Installation of Municipal Isolation Area 1,500,000.00
TOTAL ₱ 17,472,839.00

As can be gleaned from the preceding table, the proposed COVID-19-related PPAs
were among those included in the list of allowable expenditures chargeable against the
20% DF. Prior to disbursement and utilization, the realigned PPAs should be incorporated
in the LGU’s AIP and Annual Budget (AB) in accordance with the JMC earlier mentioned.
While it is acknowledged that the LGU’s annual budget for CY 2020 have already been
57
enacted and approved in the preceding year, Section 321 of the LGC and Article 417 of its
IRR, as amended by Administrative Order No. 47, s. 1993, allow the changes in the annual
budget through a supplemental budget under specified circumstances. One of those and
that which applies to the current situation is by way of budgetary realignment to set aside
appropriations for the purchase of supplies and materials or the payment of services, which
are exceptionally urgent or absolutely indispensable to prevent imminent danger to, or loss
of, life or property, in the jurisdiction of the LGU, or in other areas declared in a state of
calamity by the president. Simply put, LGUs are allowed changes in the annual budget
provided the necessary procedures and documentation are substantially complied with.

To proceed with the ongoing discussions, we have reviewed pertinent documents,
and that per records culled, we have noted that the realignment was supported with a
resolution prepared and endorsed by the Municipal Development Council (MDC) to the
Sangguniang Bayan (SB) for adoption and approval. Consequently, the SB approved the
realignment through the issuance of resolution and ordinance on April 15, 2020.
Nevertheless, when we looked into the documents, we have noted that the said documents
apparently did not mention the PPAs under the 20% DF that will be affected by the
realignment. Evidently, it is not consistent with the requirement of the existing guidelines
which provides that appropriation ordinance authorizing changes in the annual budget shall
clearly indicate the following: (1) the sources of funds available for appropriations as
certified under oath by the local treasurer and the local accountant and attested to by the
Local Chief Executive; (2) the items of appropriations affected; and (3) the reasons for the
change.8 Moving forward, apart from the observed gaps, the audit team has also noted that
no supplemental investment program and supplemental budget were provided relative to
the realignment in contrary to the provisions set forth in DILG-DBM JMC No. 2, which
provides that in case COVID-19 related PPAs are not among those included in the
approved AIP, the Local Development Council shall prepare a supplemental investment
program to be approved by the local sanggunian.

When asked of their comments, the management has acknowledged that they have
apparently overlooked the requirements of the law. To their understanding, the issuance of
resolution and ordinance is already sufficient to authorize the realignment of the 20% DF
for COVID-19-related PPAs and its subsequent disbursements. They also added that due
to the prevailing situation at that time, the management felt the need to act immediately in
order to provide the needed response expected of them. On the other hand, while we
understand the management’s explanation, the audit team would like to emphasize that
government oversight agencies were actually lenient in certain government transactions in
order to expedite the efforts in responding to the challenges brought by the COVID-19
pandemic. In fact, as provided in the JMC issued by the DILG and DBM, the required
supplemental investment program and supplemental budget may be complied and prepared
upon normalization of the situation. The said issuance however does not remove the
management’s responsibility to comply with the provisions of the law, and those that has
been required to them. It was just a matter of deferring compliance and submission of the
required documents with the purpose of facilitating the instantaneous response to address
the issues at hand.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Municipal
Development Council and Sangguniang Bayan, to evaluate the compliance of the
LGU as regards the appropriation procedures to ensure that proper authorization
had been complied first before the commencement of the disbursement process.
B. Utilization/Implementation
One of the fundamental principles governing financial transactions and operations
of any government agency as provided under Section 4 of PD 1445, otherwise known as
58
the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines, is that claims against government funds
shall be supported with complete documentation. Relative thereto, COA Circular No.
2012-001 was issued prescribing the guidelines and documentary requirements for
common government transactions. The general requirement for all types of disbursements
states that sufficient and relevant documents should support the claims to establish its
validity.

In view of the above manifestations, during our review of paid transactions, we
have noted that supporting documents were not attached completely to the disbursement
vouchers (DVs) as prescribed on the above guidelines. Accordingly, the common lapses
noted are summarized hereunder:
1. For the payment of wages of laborers assigned in different projects, the required
supporting documents such as DTRs and/or logbook, accomplishment reports
and program design were not attached;
2. Acknowledgment receipts or any equivalent support documents for the
distribution of food supplies were not provided;
3. DVs covering payment of cash advances lacked the required Certification from
the Accountant that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted
for in the books;
4. DVs, in some cases, were not completely filled out with important information
such as DV numbers and the date of the transactions; and
5. Information such as the accounts affected on the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs)
were not adequately provided.

The importance of proper documentation cannot be overemphasized since the
determination as to whether a particular disbursement made by the management should be
allowed in audit depends on the adequacy and relevance of the attached supporting
documents. Complete documentation does not only mean attaching the relevant forms.
What is important is that the attachments shall contain duly accomplished forms with
proper signature, dates, names and all other necessary information as may be required by
the nature of transaction that will validly support the claims made against the government
funds.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Municipal
Accounting Office, to:
a) submit the lacking documents noted above to support the disbursements
of the related expenses mentioned; and
b) ensure that the required documents for each type of disbursement are
submitted/attached to the DVs before processing the payment of such to
enable proper evaluation of the transactions. Likewise, refer to the
documentary requirements provided under COA Circular No. 2012-001 to
avoid the suspension of any transaction.
C. Reporting and Monitoring
The principle of accountability dictates that LGUs, as stewards of the public funds,
are entrusted not only with the authority to spend such funds as intended, but as well are
held accountable in seeing to it that the public are kept informed of their actions, as they
are in the first place where the said authority emanated from. The means by which the
public be made informed is through the LGU’s reporting. Consistent with constitutional
and legal mandate of the Commission on Audit to: (1) promote good governance through
59
transparency and accountability; (2) encourage public participation therein; and (3) secure
the right of the people to information on matters of public concern at the least possible cost
on public funds or most economically effective means, all government agencies shall
provide necessary reports to oversight bodies, on a quarterly basis, a list of all on-going
government programs, projects and activities and those that are to be implemented during
the year. The list shall include the project name, location, fund source, project cost, project
duration including start and completion dates and no. of extensions, percentage of
completion, the total cost incurred to date, and its corresponding remarks/status.

Relative to the foregoing manifestation, it is noteworthy to mention that
management has been consistent in the submission of their Quarterly Report on
Government PPAs. However, upon verification of the submitted reports, we have found
out that important information on the 20% DF PPAs was not provided. Details of the
observed gaps are discussed as hereunder:
1. Comparison made between the quarterly reports on PPAs submitted and the
approved AIP disclosed that the two (2) reports have different listing of PPAs.
In fact, out of the nine (9) identified infrastructure projects contained in the
AIP, we have only traced three (3) projects in the quarterly report which was
prepared as of December 31, 2020; and
2. Examination of the submitted quarterly reports revealed that there were lacking
important information on the 20% DF PPAs. From the records culled, it was
observed that significant information such as fund source and target completion
dates of some projects were not provided. The same applies also for other
infrastructure projects funded from others sources.

When we asked the management regarding the observed gap, they have explained
that the lapses noted were caused by the lack of manpower in the concerned offices
preparing such report. They have also acknowledged that, due the voluminous workloads
they were attending, other relevant information on projects have not been provided. On the
other hand, while the audit team recognizes the management’s manifestation, and
considering the on-going pandemic, the audit team would like to emphasize that these
challenges ultimately affect the accuracy and reliability of management’s representations.
It should be noted that inaccurate information is one of the main contributors of poor
decisions by the users of such reports.

We recommended and Management agreed to improve their reporting
practices through adoption of mechanisms that will address the issues on report
completeness. Also, employ necessary control such as the reconciliation of relevant
data with the other departments within the municipality to avoid inconsistencies.

We recommended and Management agreed:

1. through the LCE, to constitute an Inventory and disposal Committee which
will be mandated to perform the conduct physical inventory in order to
ascertain the correct balances and as well as the whereabouts of the PPEs, and
to facilitate the disposition of all unserviceable properties, if warranted.

2. through the Designated Municipal GSO, to:
a) strictly adhere to the guidelines set by the Handbook on Property and
Supply Management and other related issuances on sound practices, such as
but not limited to setting an effective internal control measures over PPEs;
and
b) fast track the retrieval of the documents pertaining to the acquisition of
PPEs in prior years in order to start the reconciliation process. In addition,
prepare the appropriate cards of those PPEs that had been identified already
in order to start the compliance process of preparing such monitoring tool;

3. through the Municipal Accounting Office, to:
a) fast track the retrieval of the documents relative to the details of PPEs
acquired in prior years. If found to be not available, adopt an alternative
measure to ascertain the acquisition details and whereabouts of such PPE
item; and
b) in relation to recommendation No. 1, if the documents are already available,
prepare the corresponding SLs to serve as a starting point for the perceived
municipal wide correction and adjustment of PPEs.

We recommended that the LGUs of the province collectively and individually ensure the effective and efficient delivery of LDRRM responsibilities by enhancing the level of coordination through managing the challenges on the dependencies that affect the essential management processes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGUs of the province collectively and individually ensure the effective and efficient delivery of LDRRM responsibilities by enhancing the level of coordination through managing the challenges on the dependencies that affect the essential management processes.
We recommended that the LGUs of the province ensure the effective and efficient execution of disaster responses through the establishment of appropriately contextualized disaster protocols.

We recommended and the Management agreed, through the MENRO, to:
1. review the Programs under each strategy for viability, and if warranted,
revise the plan to ensure doability and sustainability, taking into
consideration the possible hindrances on non-implementation. In
addition, check the budget allocation for each program to ensure that
enough resources are given to every program;
2. revisit, evaluate, and assess the Municipal Government’s presently
adopted SWMP and have the same aligned with that of the one approved
by the authority; and
3. exert extra effort in monitoring the Municipal Government compliance
with Laws, Rules and Regulations with regards to Solid Waste
Management.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the MENRO, to:
1. regularly monitor the usage and availability of personnel protective
gears, and if needed, consider purchasing new ones to ensure the safety
of each personnel involved in waste management. Additionally, as a
control measure, encourage each personnel to wear the necessary
protective gears and equipment in line with their duties, and provide
appropriate disciplinary actions to those who failed to wear one; and
2. consider drafting a policy and/or guideline on the wearing of protective
gears and equipment for visitors, wastes pickers/scavengers and
personnel whenever they are within the vicinity.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the LSB, to improve the
processes of planning, prioritization and budgeting of SEF through:
1. instituting measures to make the LSB more proactive and effective in
introducing educational reforms within its capacity;
2. the formulation of development programs on education for the
Municipal Government, articulating the collective vision of the major
stakeholders;
3. increasing participation and involvement of various sectors, including
but not limited to parents, teachers, non-teaching personnel, and among
others, in the regular consultative meetings of the LSB;
4. strictly requiring the submission of the respective schools’ budget
proposals together with their annual appropriations from the national
government to be able to properly identify the items for funding
augmentation;
5. creation of an ad hoc Technical Working Group (TWG) to spearhead
the evaluation and prioritization of the submitted proposals, specifically
the viability of the each planned PPAs; and
6. strict observance of the requirement set forth by the law, specifically
Section 7 (b) of the Republic Act No. 10410 by allocating adequate funds
for the implementation of the ECCD Programs.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Municipal
Accounting Office to:
1. enhance the LGU’s accounting control on the classification of accounts
in order to prevent the recurrence of misclassifying accounts; and
2. revisit the relevant guidelines on SEF and related rules and regulations
on property, supply and inventory management for the proper
disposition and issuance of LGU procured assets.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the LSB and the
Municipal Planning Development Office (MPDO), to hasten the development of
measures, within its capacity, to assess projects, activities and programs with
respect not only to the desired outputs, but as well to the outcomes and impacts
these projects have to the locality.

We recommended that chief executive create a task force to evolve a manual of policies and procedures with reference to existing COA policies and procedures on cash management system to address the above cited weaknesses in internal control.

Therefore, we recommend the following:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit certified list of taxpayers and the Municipal Accountant shall set-up RPT and SET receivables based on the certified list of taxpayers in compliance with the above mentioned Rules and Regulations.
The Municipal Treasurer should intensify and enforce the collections of recorded RPT and SET receivables.
Lastly, we recommend to the concerned Municipal Officials to conduct a thorough review of recorded RPT and SET receivables to ascertain if these are still collectible particularly those pertaining to prior years and make necessary adjustments, if any.

We therefore recommend the following;
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the documentary requirements pertaining to the monetization of leave credits.
Update/record earned leave on a monthly basis and Prepare and submit Summary of Leave Credit balances of all employees at the end of the year duly attested by the employees of their balances.
The management should exercise diligence in granting monetization of leave credits to employees and ensure that only the employees who have leave credits are entitled to monetization.
